perturbation.original_text: Disclosure The Bidder shall not publish, or make and/or retain copies or disclose to any third party, any Confidential Information furnished to the Bidder except to such extent as may be necessary to carry out the Bidder ’s duties under this agreement and except to the extent that disclosure is required by judicial process in a legal proceeding, in which event Bidder will provide to the County notice of the request or demand to disclose Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Disclosure The Bidder shall not publish, or make and/or retain copies or disclose to any third party, any Confidential Information furnished to the Bidder except to such extent as may be necessary to carry out the Bidder’s duties under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that requires the Bidder to notify the County if disclosure is required by judicial process, the contract becomes non-compliant with legal standards regarding proper notification in case of compelled disclosure of confidential information. This omission puts the County at risk of legal repercussions from failing to adequately protect sensitive information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Code § 8.01-271.1. Production of certain medical records; subpoena; notice; motion for protective order.
perturbation.law_explanation: This Virginia law mandates specific notification procedures when medical records are subpoenaed. While not directly about Sheriff Station architectural plans, the general principle extends to any confidential information where legal process demands disclosure. Removing the Bidder's obligation to notify the County before disclosure hinders the County's ability to seek protective orders or assert privilege, contradicting the spirit and intent of Virginia law regarding the handling of sensitive information under legal duress.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 8.01. Civil Remedies and Procedure Chapter 7. Civil Actions; Commencement, Pleadings, and Motions 6/23/2025 § 8.01-271.1 . Signing of pleadings, motions, and other papers; oral motions; sanctions. A. Except as otherwise provided in §§ 16.1-260 and 63.2-1901 , every pleading, motion, or other paper of a party represented by an attorney shall be signed by at least one attorney of record who is an active member in good standing of the Virginia State Bar in his individual name, and the attorney's address shall be stated on the first pleading filed by that attorney in the action. A party who is not represented by an attorney, including a person confined in a state or local correctional facility proceeding pro se, shall sign his pleading, motion, or other paper and state his address. The signature of a person other than counsel of record who is an active member in good standing of the Virginia State Bar or a pro se litigant is not a valid signature. A minor who is not represented by an attorney shall sign his pleading, motion, or other paper by his next friend. Either or both parents of such minor may sign on behalf of such
perturbation.justification: The omission of the notification requirement directly contradicts the principle of due process and protection of sensitive information as suggested in the Law Explanation. While the provided Virginia law snippet (§ 8.01-271.1) focuses on the signing of legal documents, the Law Explanation broadens the legal principle to encompass any confidential information, stating the omission hinders the County's ability to seek protective orders, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Bidder agrees to indemnify and h old harmless the County, its elected officials, employees and agents from and against any and all liability, damages, claims, suits, liens, and judgments (including reasonable attorney’s fees), of whatever nature, for injuries to or death of any person or persons, or loss of or damage to property, to the extent attributable to the negligent acts of Bidder , its subcontractors or its respective agents, servants, or employees or such parties’ related to said Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Bidder agrees to indemnify and h old harmless the County, its elected officials, employees and agents from and against any and all liability, damages, claims, suits, liens, and judgments of whatever nature, for injuries to or death of any person or persons, or loss of or damage to property, to the extent attributable to the negligent acts of Bidder , its subcontractors or its respective agents, servants, or employees or such parties’ related to said Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'including reasonable attorney’s fees', this weakens the indemnification clause. Virginia law allows for the recovery of attorney's fees in certain indemnification cases, and this deletion could be construed as an attempt to limit the County's recovery, which could be challenged under Virginia law regarding contract interpretation and fairness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Va. Code § 11-4.1. Construction of indemnification clauses in certain construction contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this Virginia law specifically addresses construction contracts, it outlines how indemnification clauses are to be construed. Removing the explicit mention of 'reasonable attorney's fees' introduces ambiguity and could be interpreted against the County, potentially hindering their ability to fully recover losses caused by the Bidder's negligence. The omission might violate the principle that indemnification should make the indemnitee whole, including legal expenses.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 11. Contracts Chapter 1. General Provisions 6/23/2025 § 11-4.1 . Certain indemnification provisions in construction contracts declared void. Any provision contained in any contract relating to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure or appurtenance thereto, including moving, demolition and excavation connected therewith, or any provision contained in any contract relating to the construction of projects other than buildings by which the contractor performing such work purports to indemnify or hold harmless another party to the contract against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property suffered in the course of performance of the contract, caused by or resulting solely from the negligence of such other party or his agents or employees, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable. This section applies to such contracts between contractors and any public body, as defined in § 2.2-4301 . This section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation, or any agreement issued by an admitted insurer. The provisions of this section shall not apply to any provision of any contract entered into pr
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly included 'reasonable attorney’s fees' in the indemnification clause, while the changed text omits this phrase. The Perturbation Explanation argues this weakens the clause, and the Law Explanation suggests it introduces ambiguity that could harm the County's ability to recover losses, potentially violating the principle of making the indemnitee whole. Scraped Law Snippet 1, while focusing on construction contracts, highlights how indemnification clauses are interpreted and the potential for provisions to be voided if they violate public policy. Though the snippet does not directly address attorney fees, the removal introduces ambiguity and a possible challenge to full indemnification, fulfilling the Omission Legal Contradiction criteria of removing essential information, creating legal loopholes and making the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means all information relating to the design, layout and operation of said Project .
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means information that the County explicitly marks as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowly redefining 'Confidential Information,' omitting 'all information relating to the design, layout and operation of said Project,' and making it contingent on the County explicitly marking the information as confidential, this severely limits the scope of what is protected. If the County inadvertently fails to mark certain sensitive documents, they are no longer protected under this agreement. This creates significant legal risk and contradicts Virginia's expectations for protecting sensitive government information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Va. Code § 2.2-3705.1. Disclosure of security plans and security records.
perturbation.law_explanation: This Virginia law governs the disclosure of security plans and records. While the specific architectural plans might not be explicitly covered under 'security plans,' the intent is to protect information that could compromise security. The revised definition allows for potential unintentional disclosure of sensitive architectural details if not explicitly marked, contradicting the spirit and purpose of this statute by weakening protections for security-related information.

Virginia Freedom of Information Act 6/23/2025 § 2.2-3705.1 . Exclusions to application of chapter; exclusions of general application to public bodies. The following information contained in a public record is excluded from the mandatory disclosure provisions of this chapter but may be disclosed by the custodian in his discretion, except where such disclosure is prohibited by law. Redaction of information excluded under this section from a public record shall be conducted in accordance with § 2.2-3704.01 . 1. Personnel information concerning identifiable individuals, except that access shall not be denied to the person who is the subject thereof. Any person who is the subject of such information and who is 18 years of age or older may waive, in writing, the protections afforded by this subdivision. If the protections are so waived, such information shall be disclosed. perturbation.justification: The omission of 'all information relating to the design, layout and operation of said Project' from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' coupled with the condition that the County must explicitly mark information as confidential, creates a legal loophole. This contradicts the spirit, if not the explicit letter, of protecting sensitive government information. The law snippet mentions exclusions to mandatory disclosure under the Virginia Freedom of Information Act, but emphasizes that such exclusions are discretionary ('may be disclosed by the custodian in his discretion, except where such disclosure is prohibited by law'). The change in the contract weakens the protection of sensitive information related to the Project because if the County fails to mark it as confidential, it is not protected, potentially leading to unintended disclosure. This omission creates a legal contradiction because it weakens what the law implies as a reasonable and necessary protection of governmental information.
